title: Ensure latest CNI version is used

input_row.title: Ensure latest CNI version is used
input_row.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
input_row.rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
input_row.audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports network policies. Check the DaemonSets in the kube-system namespace: Many CNI plugins operate as DaemonSets within the kube-system namespace. To see what's running: kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system Look for known CNI providers like Calico, Flannel, Cilium, etc. You can further inspect the configuration of these DaemonSets to understand more about the CNI setup: kubectl describe daemonset -n kube-system Check the CNI Configuration Files: If you have access to the nodes (via SSH), you can check the CNI configuration directly in /etc/cni/net.d/. This often requires node-level access, which might not be available depending on your permissions and the security setup of your environment.
input_row.remediation: As with RBAC policies, network policies should adhere to the policy of least privileged access. Start by creating a deny all policy that restricts all inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace or create a global policy using Calico.
input_row.default_value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. Internal Only - General
input_row.additional_information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Use the terminal or command line interface to connect to your Kubernetes cluster using `kubectl`.
2. Run the command: `kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system` to list all DaemonSets running in the kube-system namespace.
3. Identify the CNI plugin in use by looking for DaemonSets like Calico, Flannel, or Cilium. Take note of the names and configurations.
4. To gather further details, run the command: `kubectl describe daemonset <daemonset-name> -n kube-system`, replacing `<daemonset-name>` with the actual name of the identified DaemonSet.
5. Check the CNI configuration files by logging into a node in the cluster through SSH (if you have access). Run: `cat /etc/cni/net.d/*` to review the configuration.
6. Verify if the CNI plugin supports Network Policies. Refer to the relevant documentation or online resources for the particular CNI plugin identified (e.g., Calico, Flannel).
7. Document your findings, stating whether the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
8. Based on your findings, if the CNI does not comply, proceed with remediation by implementing a deny all policy for inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace and creating a global policy if using Calico.

**EXPECTED SECURE CONFIGURATION:** 
- A verified CNI plugin that supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.

**HOW TO CONFIRM COMPLIANCE:**
- Ensure that the output from your commands reflects the use of a compliant CNI plugin and that the network policies required are being enforced within the Kubernetes cluster. The confirmation can be achieved by matching the DaemonSet name against known compliant CNI providers, and by verifying the policies described in the CNI documentation. If any issues are found, take appropriate remediation actions as necessary.
